item.title: IndyCar teams come to terms on the series' first-ever charter sale, which shakes up the grid for 2027
item.description: Rahal Letterman Lanigan Racing agrees to sell one of its three IndyCar charters to Dreyer & Reinbold Racing, raising questions about Schumacher.
item.content:encoded:
The NTT IndyCar grid is going to look a little different in 2027 with news that the first sale of an IndyCar charter has been completed, and it will bring a new team to the grid.

Albeit one that fans are very familiar with.

On Wednesday, it was announced that Rahal Letterman Lanigan Racing had agreed to sell one of its three charters to Drewy & Reinbold Racing, with the transaction subject to IndyCar approval.

TRUMP TOUTS INDYCAR DRIVERS' 'SPECIAL' ABILITY AT FREEDOM 250 GRAND PRIX SHOWCASE, SEEMINGLY ENDING HOT DEBATE

It's the first sale since the charter system was introduced in 2024, and it will see RLL dropping from a three-car operation down to a two-car operation, and DRR rejoining the egrid full-time with a single car.

Dreyer & Reinbold last competed in IndyCar full-time back in 2012 and has become a regular fixture in the Indianapolis 500. They had two cars in this year's field, which were driven by Conor Daly and Jack Harvey.

This news comes just weeks after the death of DRR founder and owner Dennis Reinbold on June 13.

TRUMP TOUTS INDYCAR DRIVERS' 'SPECIAL' ABILITY AT FREEDOM 250 GRAND PRIX SHOWCASE, SEEMINGLY ENDING HOT DEBATE

"I want to thank Bobby Rahal, Mike Lanigan, David Letterman and Jay Frye for the way they approached this from our very first conversation," DRR CEO Derek Reinbold said in a statement. "They treated this as a partnership between two organizations that want to see each other succeed, and this announcement today reflects that.

The teams did not name any drivers or which of RLL's three cars' charters will head to DRR.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE OUTKICK SPORTS COVERAGE

RLL currently fields the No. 15 Honda driven by Graham Rahal, the No. 35 Honda driven by Louis Foster, and the No. 47 driven by rookie Mick Schumacher.

According to FOX Sports' Bob Pockrass, both Rahal and Foster are under contract for next season, while Schumacher is not.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

That has led to speculation that Schumacher is the odd man out. He is, however, coming off a season-best finish at Nashville.

After the race, he told Pockrass that he feels there's a high chance he will be on the grid in 2027.

We'll see how it shakes out, because this has already been one of the crazier IndyCar silly seasons in recent memory, with even the likes of Christian Lundgaard — who has two wins on the year and his fifth in the standings — hunting for a new ride for 2027.

item.title: Memphis pizza shop says business is booming after refusing service to National Guard troops
item.description: Miles Tamboli says the Memphis Safe Task Force makes the city less safe, and his pizza shop is seeing a surge of local supporters after the boycott call.
item.content:encoded:
A Memphis pizza restaurant that drew national attention after refusing to serve National Guard members says business is booming instead of busting, with supporters flocking to the restaurant in what some have dubbed a "buy-cott" after conservative activists called for a boycott.

Miles Tamboli, owner of Tamboli's Pasta & Pizza, said he refused to serve the Guardsmen on July 11 because he opposes the federally led Memphis Safe Task Force, arguing the use of National Guard troops in local law enforcement has made the city less safe rather than more secure.

The company faced severe backlash online after refusing service to four uniformed members of the Memphis Safe Task Force. Tamboli told Fox News Digital July 15, "I stand behind that decision completely." The Guardian reported Tuesday that the response locally has been supportive.

MEMPHIS PIZZA JOINT SPARKS BACKLASH AFTER OWNER REFUSES TO SERVE NATIONAL GUARD TROOPS

"The Italian 'junt' (in Memphis parlance) opened with a full house and had a line waiting by 5.30pm," The Guardian reported. "Waitstaff said the shop was never that crowded that early; extra crew was brought in to help. Business is up ‘a great amount,’ Tamboli said. The packed house on Friday was evidence."

One customer, Sarah Jones, said, "While I do not blame the National Guard for being in Memphis since it was not their choice, I stand by businesses refusing service as a form of resistance and protest."

Another customer, Carol Bigam, a retired FedEx worker, said, "If you have a business — a small family-owned business — that says, ‘You know what, I’m sorry, I can’t serve you, I just don’t believe you should be here,’ it really should be OK. Is he not allowed free speech? He was very professional about it. Give me a break, man."

ICE RESUMES NATIONWIDE TRAFFIC STOPS UNDER NEW POLICY REQUIRING BODY CAMERAS

Tamboli told Fox News Digital July 15 that he opposes the Memphis Safe Task Force, a federally led initiative established in September 2025 by President Donald Trump to stop violent crime in the city.

The task force combines local police with agents from various federal agencies, the Tennessee National Guard and state troopers to enforce public safety laws.

"What the Task Force has actually done is make this city harder to live in," Tamboli said. "Its own records show that the overwhelming majority of its arrests began with routine traffic stops, not violent crime. Families in this city are now afraid to drive to work, afraid to take their kids to school, afraid to be seen. Our own schools reported that fear drove children to stop showing up to class.

"And this month, a 20-year-old Memphian named Tyrin Johnson was shot and killed by National Guard troops during a foot chase, with no body camera footage and no answers for his family," he continued. "None of that makes us safer. It makes us less safe, and it does the most damage to the people who were already struggling."

BAKERY OWNER WHO WENT VIRAL WITH RESPONSE TO CRITICAL INFLUENCER SPEAKS OUT ON WHY SHE DEFENDED HER BUSINESS

In a statement to Fox News Digital, Tamboli said, "Business the week after roughly doubled our normal volume. It was the busiest stretch we've had in this building, with a line out front before we opened most nights. I hear the real question in yours, whether this is a blip, and it isn't. The support hasn't slowed down, I expect it to last, and I'm running my business like the lasting increase it is. The records exist, and I stand behind every word of this, and I'm not going to turn my private sales reports into a public document."

He said he does not believe conservative customers have stopped coming to his restaurant.

"I see every message, every email and every phone call that reaches this place, and I have not heard from a single regular customer telling me they won't be back," Tamboli said. "Not one. I don't track anyone's politics and my register doesn't record it, and I serve every guest who walks through my door the way I always have. The one exception is the uniformed deployment, and that was the entire point.

"Here is what I'd rather you spend your ink on. Four people are dead at the hands of this task force since it came to Memphis, in a city where violent crime had already fallen to a 25-year low before they arrived. One of them, Tyrin Johnson, was 20 years old. Another, Jonah Neal, was in the middle of a mental health crisis when a federal agent killed him, and I'd bet most of your readers have never heard his name.

"If a president sent soldiers and federal agents into your town, and four of your neighbors ended up dead, you would not be asking a pizza owner about his Friday sales," Tamboli continued. "You would be asking why it happened, and you would be furious and you would be right to be. That is the outrage this deserves, and it is missing only because it is happening here and not somewhere the country is used to watching.

"A small business owner using his First Amendment right to run his place by his own conscience is about as American as it gets," he added. "The people trying to punish that speech, the ones review-bombing me and the ones cheering the government on, are the ones who walked away from the Constitution they claim to love. You do not get to wave the flag and boo free speech in the same breath."

item.title: Wisconsin woman's honeymoon ruined by diarrhea after she came down with Cyclosporiasis
item.description: Newlywed Gretchen Pleuss caught cyclosporiasis while camping at Yellowstone National Park, landing her in the hospital with a parasite mid-honeymoon.
item.content:encoded:
We all have stuff that comes up in our lives that we file away in a keep-to-ourselves section of our brain. Having a horrible case of diarrhea that ruins your honeymoon isn't one of them.

That's a story you not only tell everyone, it's one that you run out and make sure the news is aware of. Otherwise, you're faking it through answering the question "how was your honeymoon?"

Nobody wants to put themselves in that situation. The moment you let it slip to one person that you had diarrhea so bad you ended up in the hospital, everyone is going to want to know about it.

STOMACH BUGS SURGING AS DOCTORS SAY CERTAIN TRAVEL ESSENTIALS COULD SAVE YOUR TRIP

Getting the news involved makes that far less awkward of a situation. Now, instead of your friends and family being the only ones who know that your marriage got off to a crappy start, the whole world can learn about it.

It's some personal housekeeping mixed with a public service announcement. Newlywed Gretchen Pleuss is the hero most of us aren't brave enough to be.

She caught Cyclosporiasis during her honeymoon at Yellowstone National Park and a couple of days into the camping trip was glued to the toilet with a bad case of the runs.

"It was just like incessant diarrhea. It was watery and constant," she told 12 WISN. "I felt fine until we got out here, and a couple days into camping here, I started having symptoms."

CYCLOSPORA OUTBREAK: IS IT STILL SAFE TO EAT AT RESTAURANTS? HERE'S WHAT TO KNOW

Pleuss' symptoms included stomach pain that got so bad it landed her in the hospital. That's where doctors confirmed that she had the Cyclospora parasite.

She wasn't about to let the incessant diarrhea keep her from trying to enjoy her honeymoon. Sure, it was ruined by constant trips to the bathroom and a visit to the hospital, but the couple was staying positive.

"It's still our honeymoon, so we're making the best of it," Pleuss said. "Our vows said in sickness and in health, so we're just getting an early start on that."

They're also getting an early start on having some stories to tell. For as long as they're married, they'll always remember their diarrhea-filled honeymoon, a marital test right out of the gate.

item.title: Charlie Woods' shot at the U.S. Junior Amateur ruined by clueless golf fan's phone: 'Really, dude?'
item.description: A camera click from a fan's phone disrupted Charlie Woods' concentration in the rough during a crucial U.S. Junior Amateur playoff at Saucon Valley.
item.content:encoded:
At least he didn't confiscate the phone like Rory.

Charlie Woods, the son of Tiger Woods, attempted to win the 2026 U.S. Junior Amateur ended on Wednesday during a 14-for-2 playoff at Saucon Valley Country Club. But a camera click on a fan's phone threw off Woods' concentration, causing him to chunk a shot out of the thick rough.

According to Golf Channel, the incident took place on the first hole of the playoff which was to determine the final two match-play participants. "Really, dude?" Charlie, 17, responded after hitting his third shot on the Par 5 from the deep stuff.

JORDAN SPIETH SAYS GAMBLING IS FUELING ROWDY GOLF CROWDS AND WANTS THE SPORT TO ADDRESS IT SOON

Charlie was left with a 35-foot chip for birdie, but he finished with a bogey and his dream of winning the Junior Amateur was over after carding a +5 over two days.

If there's good news from this situation, it's that the Charlie Woods' detractors didn't get another f-bomb to use against him like they got on Tuesday when Tiger took a page out of his playbook by dropping a massive f-bomb on the par-3 11th hole.

"Oh, f–k you," Charlie responded to his tee shot. It's unclear what the problem was with the shot. He made par on the hole.

SERGIO GARCIA DESTROYS HIS DRIVER IN FRUSTRATION AT THE MASTERS, RECEIVES CODE OF CONDUCT WARNING

As for the phone going off, the golf community seems to be split on how this played out. "Golfers are so lame. Guys can hit 95mph cut fast balls with 50,000 people screaming. A little noise doesn't impact your ability to swing a stupid golf club at a ball that isn't moving," one Twitter analyst observed.

There seems to be a deep belief that Tiger needs to drill Charlie on how to battle through such nonsense. "Tiger's Dad would have berated Tiger if he let a camera click affect him...He used to do everything possible to piss Tiger off during a round to get him to focus," another fan noted.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

It's not like Tiger hasn't tried to rattle his son for moments like he faced Wednesday. Remember, Earl Woods famously used to throw golf balls and coins at his son to throw off his concentration. Charlie has faced similar warfare.

TIGER WOODS BREAKS MONTHS-LONG SILENCE WITH PATRIOTIC MEMORIAL DAY MESSAGE, SALUTE TO LATE FATHER

However, after the way this Junior Amateur ended, Tiger needs to start snapping his iPhone camera in his son's backswing. It's back to the drawing board for the Woods' family.
